title: How LLM Datasets Are Made: Inside FineWeb
transcript: Good morning everyone, how's it going today? Today we're going to be talking about the FineWeb dataset. Now this is a project by Hugging Face and it is a great example of how to gather all the data that you need to train a large language model. This actually is also available in the form of a very nice blog post and it is very educational. If you want to read more about it after watching this video, feel free to read this post. it is very, very well made, and there is a lot of very useful information, and there is, of course, a paper as well. Now, this one right here dates from a couple years ago, but the information is still pretty relevant, and it teaches you a lot about how large language models nowadays are made. The slides of this are also in the description in case you want to give a talk about this later on. So, without any further ado, let's actually get right into it. Okay, so first of all, why would we talk about the FineWeb dataset anyways? Why is this relevant? And the reason for this is that there is very little information on the web about how to create data sets to train these large language models. Precisely because even if a company that creates large language models publishes these models open source, they rarely publish their pre-training data as well. because there is just very little incentive to do that. So we set out to create an open data set that is very good and that trains very good large language models and we documented pretty much every single step of the process of creating this to show you how you can do this yourself. And what we're going to be talking is mainly two flavors of this data set which is first of all the FineWeb, which is the regular FineWeb dataset, which is a 15 trillion token dataset in English derived from Common Crawl dumps. And we're going to be talking about every single step that we took to create this dataset right here. And after that, we're going to talk very quickly also about the FineWeb-Edu dataset, which is a more distilled version of the FineWeb dataset in which we used some open models to extract only the most valuable and educational data from this data set and ended up with a 1.3 trillion token data set that actually performs even better than this 15 trillion token data set So yeah we going to be talking about both of them The core idea behind the FineWeb is similar to the contention So we are going to be starting with a very large corpus of text, which comes from the Common Crawl doms, which is just a lot of raw web data. And then we just going to apply a series of transformations and a series of cleaning steps to make it a refined FineWeb dataset. And then on top of that, we're going to refine it even more to get to the FineWeb-Edu data set. All right, so let's start off with the first part, which is actually getting the data from the web. You probably have heard that large language models are trained on a huge corpus of data that comes from the entire web. Now, how do you do that? There are, of course, multiple ways, and different companies do this differently. Some, for example, like OpenAI or Anthropic, they crawl the web and scrape the web themselves to get their own extracts of the web. What we do, or what we did right here, is we actually took the snapshots from Common Crawl. Now, Common Crawl is an open source, sorry, open non-profit organization that publishes snapshots of the entire web every one or two months, and they have been doing that for years. So the entry of the starting point for this data set is precisely these snapshots from Common Crawl. So we started with 96 snapshots released since 2013, and each snapshot contains hundreds of terabytes of raw HTML data. Now, as you can imagine, there is going to be a lot, a lot of data in here, and a lot of it is going to be very dirty. In other words, there is going to be a lot of code or a lot of contents in this data that is going to be basically just boilerplate, spam, navigation menus, duplicated pages, or also non-English text. So what we did is we extracted that data and tried to clean it. And there are basically two approaches to do this. What Common Crawl actually does is that it allows you to download these extracts or these snapshots of the web into different formats. One is WARC files which stands for Web Archive Files which is the raw HTML data It is of course more expensive to process because you have to clean it yourself Or they also offer a type of files that are already extracted So there is supposedly no HTML in there. So it's only text-only files extracted by Common Crawl themselves. It is, of course, cheaper and simpler and simpler to process. But in our tests, we figured that training tiny models on this data set actually performed worse than on this data set plus our own cleaning methods. So what we did is we downloaded the raw HTML and metadata files and we used Trafilatura to extract the text from these. And just for the record this is probably the most expensive part of the entire process. So for smaller teams who are trying to do something like this, this bot right here might be one that they can skip although we did find that as you can see right here when training a tiny model on this data set and another one with the same architecture on this data set right here we ended up getting much better results with our own cleaned up version so that was the first step all right so how do we approach the cleaning and filtering of this huge corpus of already extracted data. Ideally, we already have these huge data sets, huge snapshots that are not in raw HTML, but in nice, beautiful text. But we still have to clean and remove content that we do not want to train our models on. And in order to do that, we did some very standard base filtering. First of all, we removed adult content using a URL block list. We also removed all of the data in this data set that is not in English. There is, by the way, a second version of this same data set, which is multilingual. I will leave a link in the description to that one as well. But in this particular one, we're dealing with only English data. And then we just added some extra quality filters for repetition to make sure that we do not have too much repeated data. The result after the whole thing was that we ended up with about 36 trillion tokens. This is just the first step of filtering. Now we're getting to the more tricky part of the process, which is the duplication Now the web is of course full of repeated content There are mirrors there are scraped copies there are templates there are syndicated articles there are recrawled pages and all of that just makes for a lot of repeated content in our dataset So we have to deduplicate. Why? Well, the duplication is a process that many teams use very often because it improves model generalization, it reduces memorization of your model, and also, very importantly, you avoid wasting training steps on repeated text, which means that you don't end up spending extra resources retraining your model on text that it has already seen. and basically just increases data diversity. So we used MinHash to deduplicate this huge corpus of text against itself. And the first approach that we took was actually a failed one because we assumed that more deduplication was better. So what we did at first is we deduplicated the entire dataset against itself. and the way to do this is remember that we have about 96 snapshots of the web that we got from Common Crawl and what we did is first of all we took the first snapshot or the most recent one and we deduplicated it against itself then we went to the previous one and we deduplicated that one against the newer one that was already deduplicated then this new deduplicated crawl, we used it to deduplicate against the older one, and then again the older one, and again the older one, all the way until the last one. And by the oldest crawls, since they, of course, are being deduplicated against the entire set of text from the previous Common Crawl extracts, this process was removing about 90% of the data from the oldest ones, which of course can be either very good or very bad. But we ended up with 4 trillion tokens instead of the 36 trillion tokens that we started off with. And that wouldn't be too much of a problem if the quality improved, but it did not. So when we trained a small model on both the the duplicated dataset and the non-duplicated dataset, we found out that actually the results were pretty much the same. So we did not improve at all on our performance. We certainly did not get closer to our standard that we set out to achieve, which is the refined web dataset. Now, if you're not familiar with the refined web dataset, it is the dataset that is open and it was used to train the Falcon model. So what happened here? Why did global deduplication actually not work? we figured that we, in the end, ended up removing very valuable data from all of these older snapshots. And in order to figure out, we took one of the older snapshots of this data set, and we trained a small model on the portion of the data that was removed, and then another one with the same architecture on the portion of the data that remained. and we figured and we found out that the model that was trained on the removed data actually performed better than the model that was trained on the data that was kept in this very old snapshot. So basically what that meant is that we were removing very valuable data from our older snapshots by this very aggressive deduplication. And upon closer inspection, we actually found out that the data that remained after all of this was basically just template data, navigation data, ads, and stuff like that. So this was definitely not the way to go. So we changed the approach and we used the duplicate and we used the duplication within each dump and then we just added all those dumps together. So for the latest dump, we deduplicated it against itself. Then the previous one, we deduplicated it only against itself, not against the newer one. And every single one, we just duplicated it within itself. We kept the text. And then by the end, we have this nicer FineWeb dataset with about 15 trillion tokens. And this actually ended up performing much, much better. So now we have this FineWeb dataset with independent minhash, which finally reaches the same performance as the refined web data set. And as you can see here are our previous attempts and we are performing much much better now And just for the record in case this is not clear what these plots are measuring is the aggregate score of tiny models that we train with the same architecture and same design on these different datasets And then we just evaluate these models among different benchmarks, such as NMLU. and then we aggregate the score on all of those benchmarks and then we plot that right here. That is how we measure how good a dataset is. And we finally got to the point where we have a dataset that is comparable to the refined web dataset. So pretty good. After this, we added some additional quality filtering. So we compared our FineWeb dataset towards a very strong baseline, which is the C4 dataset. Now the C4 dataset is a very well-performing dataset that is actually very impressive because it achieves incredible results by just applying four very strict rules to its dataset and just removing every text or every piece of data that does not pass these rules. so first of all it removes documents with bad length profiles or essentially documents that do not end in a punctuation mark so if you have a document in your data set for which the phrases do not end in a punctuation mark then the document is removed then it also removes documents containing any lorem ipsum which in other words is just template text it removes all the documents that talk about JavaScript or cookie notice. And then finally, we remove all the documents that contain curly braces. And just by doing that, the C4 dataset actually achieves great, great results. So what we did is we took these filters and we applied them to the FineWeb dataset. And we found out that this particular filter, the one that filtered out the documents with sentences that did not end in a punctuation mark, just that one, just that filter actually ended up removing about 30% of our tokens, which we figured was too destructive. So what we did is we applied all of the other rules of the C4 dataset but not that one not the one that removed the documents without final punctuation mark So what we did is we replaced that with our custom rules And these custom rules are these three that you see right here. First of all, we remove documents where too few lines end with punctuation. So instead of removing every single line, we have a threshold of 12%. So when 12% of the lines do not end with a punctuation mark, then we remove the document. That also gives us a little bit more room to keep text that is actually well written, but sometimes does not end with a punctuation mark. We remove documents with many characters in duplicated lines. So the threshold was more than 10% duplicated content across the documents. And also we remove documents documented, dominated by very short lines. So if a document had more than 67% of the lines on their 30 characters we removed that document from the data set and after doing this we removed 22% of the tokens instead of 30% that we would have removed if we had used the just the C4 rules and on top of that we improved the benchmark performance as you can see right here so we have our bench our baseline right here at the bottom and all of the filters combined ended up performing much much better and when we compare this final version of the FineWeb dataset you can see that it is much better than other open data sets online and we actually ended up improving quite substantially over both refined web and c4 so we're already pretty well right here and there you go that is basically what we did to train the FineWeb data just to give you a very quick recap first of all we extracted 96 Common Crawl work snapshots and we cleaned them ourselves using Trapiladora then we just cleaned them using url based filtering language filtering and some very quick quality filters we deduplicated within each crawl not against the entire data set and then we added some c4 style and custom filters to remove more data and we ended up with a 15 trillion token dataset that actually performs better than all of the other open datasets available at the time. Okay so let talk very quickly about the FineWebEDU dataset And this is a very fun example of how you can use open models in the pipeline to improve on your existing data And so why did we create FineWebEDU First of all Lama 3 and Phi 3 at the time reportedly using educational quality filtering. In other words, they took a huge dataset and they filtered for only the documents that had the highest educational value. However, their classifiers were not public. So what we did is we trained our own classifier and we ended up getting much better results in this much smaller FineWeb-Edu because remember that this one right here is just above 1 trillion tokens, whereas FineWeb is 15 trillion tokens. And this one right here actually ends up getting better results in a bunch of educational heavy benchmark. So let's take a look at how they did it. So the first thing that we did is we used a huge and very powerful model as an annotator. So we used Lama 370B Instruct, which is a huge model, and we used it to score about 500,000 FineWeb samples of 500,000 documents in our FineWeb data set and score them from zero to five depending on their educational value. So zero would be zero educational value. 5 would be something like PhD level educational value. And we of course asked the model to not just give us the score like that, but we gave it a bunch of very detailed rubrics that it had to argument on and had to score on. And then in the end, just add the whole thing to give us a score between 0 and 5. And that of course reduces hallucination to make sure that The model does not just hallucinate a random number between 0 and 5 for a given document. And we used this huge 70 billion parameter model to score all of these data points. But we would not have been able to just release this huge model onto a 15 trillion token data set. So what we did is we labeled 1500,000 data points with LAMA 370B. And then we trained a smaller model on those already scored documents. And it was this smaller model that we used in, uh, that we released in the entire FineWeb dataset and we got models. much better results by running a much smaller model. And in the end, we had a very well scored 15 trillion token dataset. And now we had to choose which threshold were we going to define to keep or remove documents from our data. And there were many different options we could have done. We could have removed anything below the score of 2 or anything below the score of three or anything below the score of four, we ended up deciding on removing every document with a score lower than three. And that gave us educational documents that were not only of university level, but also high school or secondary high school level right here, which is already pretty good. And it gave us about 1.3 trillion tokens. And when we tested this smaller 1.3 trillion token dataset, against the other data sets that we have right here. So for example, we have RefinedWeb, we have FindWeb, we have C4 on educational benchmarks such as MMLU. We found out that FindWeb with that tiny amount of data actually ended up training much better models as you can see right here. And to me, that is just crazy. And here you have the same FineWeb-Edu data set, and it is compared against the FineWeb data set that we just created, the RefineWeb, the C4, and other open data sets online. And you can see that FineWeb-Edu actually ends up just destroying the other data sets and their aggregate score. So what are the main lessons for today? First of all, extraction quality matters. So we preferred using the raw format of the Common Crawl snapshots rather than the readily available wet text because it improved on the quality of our dataset. Also, that deduplication is not monotonic. You should not deduplicate the entire thing against itself because a more aggressive deduplication can hurt your data set Evaluate with models not only heuristics So filters were selected via ablation runs. In other words, and evaluate with models, not only heuristics. What we did, as I mentioned a couple of times during the video, is that we trained the data sets on small models and then we evaluated those models to see how good our data set is. That is pretty much the best way to figure out if your data set is good or not, because that is what these data sets are supposed to be used for, to train models. So by evaluating the models on those data sets, we effectively evaluate the data sets. And also something very interesting that we found out is that data quality varies over time, as I'm going to mention in just a moment. because we're going to see that later or more recent data snapshots are actually of different quality than older ones. And we're going to see a little bit more about that. And finally, that big large language models can bootstrap scalable filters. In other words, it is very possible to use a huge and very powerful large language model to label or to score a small section of your data set and then train a smaller model on that, which is the one that you're going to release onto your full dataset. And just for quick record, here you have the entire pipeline for both of these datasets. Remember that for FineWeb, we started with Common Crawl, then Work plus Traffillatura, the base filters of language and quality. Then we deduplicated, and finally we had the 15 trillion token dataset. And for the FineWeb-Edu, we started off with a FineWeb dataset we labeled 1500 000 samples from it with lemma 370b we trained a small classifier on this samples right here and then we selected for educational content with a threshold of three out of five so we ended up with a 1.3 trillion data set when the threshold is three or more as a score and which ended up getting much better results and just Just before we leave I wanted to give you a very quick bonus piece of information right here that is very fun and very nice to look at, which is the fact that Common Crawl snapshots actually vary in quality over time. And that is, I mean, pretty crazy in my opinion. So what's going on is that the quality of the data that we get from the web dictates how good our models are going to be, right? So that is why we did all of this filtering and all of this quality filtering, the duplication, and all of this education filtering, etc. So what happens right here is just a very quick part of the experiment is that we took all of these extracts or snapshots from Common Crawl. I remember that we have one about every two or two months, one or two months. And we train a small model on every single one of those snapshots. and we ended up finding out that the latest snapshots actually produce much better models so recent dumps are not just more data they're often better data and we started wondering about why that is the case and as you can see right here this increase in quality starts coming right here about 2020-22 and I don't know about you but I can think of a very interesting thing that happened about this time right here in 2022. So we started wondering, would it be the case that the reason for this increase in quality is that we have more synthetic data on the web? In other words, we have more data generated by large language models. And it turns out that most likely, yes. It is very difficult, of course, to predict or to classify a piece of text to figure out whether or not it was generated by a large language model. But we figured out a very quick proxy measurement to do this. We basically monitored some words and phrases that are very commonly used by large language models, such as delve into certainly and stuff like that. And we measured how often they appeared in our previous snapshots And as you can see before 2023 the frequency of those words remained relatively constant And after 2023, we started with this explosion of those LLM signature words, which is, of course, not a proof that this is LLM-generated data, but it does give us a pretty good hint that this might be generated by ChatGPT or a similar large language model. So this basically gives us a very interesting finding, which is that snapshots that have a lot of synthetic data end up producing better models. At least that is the case right now, where we have a tiny fraction of the snapshots being LLM-generated data. Now the question is, what is going to happen when the larger part of the snapshots is synthetic data generated by large language models? We'll have to figure it out as that happens. And by the end, let's just close this with just some open questions, which is, as I was telling you, will synthetic data make the web data worse? So right now we have a lot of synthetic data in the web, but not too much. Will having too much of this synthetic data harm the quality of large language models that we get in the future? And the second thing that I want to mention is that this is, of course, a little bit of an older article that I'm presenting right here. So right now we have even better small models than the LAMA370B. So this is a 70 billion parameter model that was very good at the time, but we now have much better models that are sometimes below 20 billion parameters. So will the use of these smaller models allow us to create much more sophisticated data pipelines for classification and for synthetic data generation even? That is basically all for this presentation. Thank you very much. Of course, the slides for this are in the description, and if you have any questions feel free to leave them as well in the comments thank you and have a great one
